title: Bubble Sort: Watch Numbers Float Up
subtitle: Physically swap cards on your desk to see exactly how bubble sort works.
encouragement: Sorting algorithms were first studied in the 1950s by computer science pioneers. Bubble sort is one of the simplest to understand, and once you see it with your hands, you'll never forget it.
realWorldExample: Imagine sorting a hand of playing cards by repeatedly comparing two neighbors and swapping them if they're out of order. That's exactly bubble sort.

materials.detail: Write one number on each: 5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3. Any scrap paper works; cut or tear it into roughly equal pieces.
To keep track of how many comparisons and swaps you make.

setup: Lay your 6 cards in a row from left to right: 5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3
Leave a little space between each card so you can swap them easily.
If you have scrap paper, draw two columns labeled "Comparisons" and "Swaps"; make a tally mark for each one as you go.

steps.instruction:
PASS 1. Here's the one rule of bubble sort: Compare two neighbors. If the left one is bigger, swap them. Start from the very left.

Compare 5 and 2. Is 5 > 2? Yes! Swap them.
steps.conceptNote: In code: if lst[i] > lst[i+1]: lst[i], lst[i+1] = lst[i+1], lst[i]. One comparison, one conditional swap.

interaction.label: 5 > 2? Yes! Tap both highlighted cards to swap.

steps.instruction: Compare 5 and 8. Is 5 > 8? No. Leave them.
steps.conceptNote: Not every comparison results in a swap. We only swap when the left neighbor is larger.

steps.hint: Notice how 8 is "moving" to the right. It's bubbling up toward its correct position.

steps.instruction: Compare 8 and 9. Is 8 > 9? No. Leave them.

steps.checkpoint: End of Pass 1: 2, 5, 1, 8, 3, 9. The largest number (9) has bubbled to the end!
steps.conceptNote: After one complete pass, the largest number is guaranteed to be in its correct final position. Mark 9 as DONE.
steps.hint: It's called "bubble" sort because the largest values "bubble up" to the right end, like air bubbles rising in water.

steps.instruction:
PASS 2. Back to the left. Stop before 9 (it's already placed).

Compare 2 and 5. Is 2 > 5? No.
steps.conceptNote: Each pass does one fewer comparison because one more element is in its final spot.

steps.checkpoint: End of Pass 2: 2, 1, 5, 3, 8, 9. Now 8 AND 9 are in their final spots.

steps.instruction:
PASS 3. Back to the left. Stop before 8 and 9.

Compare 2 and 1. Is 2 > 1? Yes! Swap.

steps.checkpoint: End of Pass 3: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9. Now 5, 8, and 9 are placed.
steps.hint: The list looks sorted already! But the algorithm doesn't "know" that yet. It will do one more pass to confirm.

steps.instruction:
PASS 4. Back to the left. Compare 1 and 2: No swap. Compare 2 and 3: No swap.

Zero swaps in this entire pass. That means the list is sorted!
steps.conceptNote: Optimization: if an entire pass makes ZERO swaps, the list is already sorted and we can stop early. In code, use a swapped = False flag.

steps.instruction: Let's count the work. How many total comparisons and swaps did you make?
steps.checkpoint:
Pass 1: 5 comparisons, 3 swaps. Pass 2: 4 comparisons, 2 swaps. Pass 3: 3 comparisons, 2 swaps. Pass 4: 2 comparisons, 0 swaps.

Total: 14 comparisons, 7 swaps.
steps.conceptNote: For n items, worst case is (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 comparisons. For n = 6, that's 15. We did 14 because we stopped one pass early. This is why bubble sort is O(n²).
steps.hint: O(n²) means if you double the number of items, the work roughly quadruples. Bubble sort is simple but slow for large lists.

steps.instruction: Try it yourself! Shuffle your cards back to a random order and do bubble sort again from scratch. This time, count your swaps and try the early-stop optimization.
steps.action: If a full pass has zero swaps, stop immediately; the list is sorted!
steps.conceptNote: Best case: the list is already sorted. You do one pass with 0 swaps and stop. That's O(n), just n-1 comparisons. Worst case (reverse sorted): every pair swaps every pass. That's O(n²).

reflection.question: Why does the largest unsorted number always end up at the right after each pass?
reflection.answer: Because we compare neighbors left to right, the biggest number always "wins" every comparison and keeps getting swapped to the right until it reaches the end of the unsorted section.

reflection.question: What would happen if the list started already sorted? How many passes would you need?
reflection.answer: Just one pass with zero swaps. The early-stop optimization detects this and stops. That's the best case: O(n).

reflection.question: What if the list was in reverse order (9, 8, 5, 3, 2, 1)? Would that be more work? Why?
reflection.answer: Yes, that's the worst case! Every pair is out of order, so every comparison results in a swap. You need the maximum number of passes with the maximum swaps each time.

reflection.question: Could you sort the list by bubbling the SMALLEST to the left instead? What would change?
reflection.answer: Yes! You'd compare right to left instead, and the smallest value would "sink" to the left each pass. The algorithm is the same idea in reverse, with the same O(n²) runtime.
